pillar.h1: Continuing Care Retirement Community Near Me: Your Guide to CCRCs & Life Plan Communities
pillar.quickAnswer: A continuing care retirement community (CCRC), also called a Life Plan Community, offers independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing on one campus, so you never have to move as your needs change. The U.S. has roughly 1,900 CCRCs (LeadingAge). To find one near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.heroSubhead: Looking for a continuing care retirement community near you? A CCRC, or Life Plan Community, lets you age in place with independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing on a single campus. Our team helps you understand entrance fees, contract types, and Medicare coverage at no cost — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
pillar.intro: Continuing care retirement communities let seniors settle in one place and access more care as they need it. This guide explains how CCRCs work, what they cost, and how to find the right one near you.

sections.h2: What is a continuing care retirement community (CCRC)?
sections.body: A continuing care retirement community (CCRC) is a senior housing campus that combines several levels of care in one location: independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing. Residents move in while still active and independent, then transition to higher levels of care on the same campus if their health changes — without relocating to a new community. Many providers now use the term "Life Plan Community" to emphasize this continuum. CCRCs typically charge a one-time entrance fee plus monthly fees. There are about 1,900 CCRCs nationwide (LeadingAge). To compare options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do I find a CCRC or Life Plan Community near me?
sections.body: To find a CCRC near you, start by deciding your priorities: location near family, contract type, entrance-fee budget, and the care levels offered on campus. Tour at least two or three communities, ask to see the most recent financial disclosure statement, and confirm the community is licensed in your state. Accreditation by CARF (the Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities) is a strong quality signal — fewer than 15% of CCRCs are CARF-accredited. Our senior advisors can shortlist communities in your area and explain the fine print. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, no-pressure help.

sections.h2: What does a CCRC cost?
sections.body: CCRC costs have two parts: a one-time entrance fee and an ongoing monthly fee. Entrance fees commonly range from about $40,000 to over $2 million, with many falling between $300,000 and $600,000, depending on the residence, location, and contract type (AARP and industry data). Monthly fees typically run from roughly $2,000 to $6,000 and cover housing, services, and a portion of future care. The contract type you choose — Type A, B, or C — heavily affects both the upfront and ongoing price. To understand what a community in your area would cost, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Does Medicare or Medicare Supplement insurance pay for a CCRC?
sections.body: Medicare does not pay CCRC entrance fees or monthly fees — those are housing and personal-care costs. Medicare does cover medically necessary services within a CCRC the same way it does anywhere else: hospital stays (Part A), doctor visits (Part B), short-term skilled nursing rehab after a qualifying hospital stay, and prescription drugs (Part D). A Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan can pay the deductibles and coinsurance Original Medicare leaves behind, protecting your savings as you move through a CCRC's care levels. To review your Medicare and Medigap coverage before choosing a community, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: CCRC contract types: Type A, Type B, and Type C explained
sections.body: CCRCs offer three main contracts. Type A (Life Care) charges higher upfront and monthly fees but locks in little or no increase when you need assisted living or nursing care — essentially prepaid care. Type B (Modified) costs less initially and includes a set amount of higher-level care, after which you pay discounted rates. Type C (Fee-for-Service) has the lowest entrance fee but you pay full market rate for each care level as you use it. The right contract depends on your health outlook and finances. A licensed advisor can walk you through the trade-offs — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What is a continuing care retirement community?
faqs.a: A continuing care retirement community (CCRC), or Life Plan Community, is a campus offering independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing in one place. Residents move in independently and access higher care levels as needed without relocating. They typically charge an entrance fee plus monthly fees.

faqs.q: How much does a CCRC cost?
faqs.a: CCRC entrance fees commonly range from about $40,000 to over $2 million, with many between $300,000 and $600,000, plus monthly fees of roughly $2,000 to $6,000. Costs vary by location, residence size, and whether you choose a Type A, B, or C contract. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: Is a CCRC the same as a Life Plan Community?
faqs.a: Yes. "Life Plan Community" is the modern industry term for a continuing care retirement community (CCRC). The name change, adopted by LeadingAge in 2015, emphasizes planning for the future. Both describe the same model: multiple care levels on one campus so residents can age in place.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for a CCRC?
faqs.a: Medicare does not pay CCRC entrance or monthly fees, which are housing and personal-care costs. Medicare does cover medically necessary care within a CCRC — hospital stays, doctor visits, and short-term skilled nursing rehab. A Medicare Supplement plan can cover the gaps Original Medicare leaves. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your coverage.

faqs.q: Are CCRC entrance fees refundable?
faqs.a: It depends on the contract. Many CCRCs offer refundable plans returning 50%, 75%, or 90% of the entrance fee to you or your estate, usually after the unit is re-occupied. Fully declining (non-refundable) plans cost less upfront but return nothing over time. Always read the residency agreement carefully.

faqs.q: What's the difference between a CCRC and assisted living?
faqs.a: Assisted living provides one level of care — help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, and medication. A CCRC includes assisted living plus independent living, memory care, and skilled nursing on the same campus, so residents can transition between care levels without moving to a new facility.

faqs.q: How many CCRCs are there in the United States?
faqs.a: There are roughly 1,900 continuing care retirement communities across the United States, serving an estimated 900,000-plus older adults, according to LeadingAge and industry sources. They range from small nonprofit communities to large for-profit campuses, and availability varies widely by state and metro area.

faqs.q: How do I choose the right CCRC near me?
faqs.a: Compare location, contract type, entrance-fee budget, and the care levels offered. Tour several communities, review the latest financial disclosure statement, confirm state licensing, and look for CARF accreditation. A licensed senior advisor can shortlist options and explain the fine print — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.

page.intro: Finding the right CCRC near you means weighing location, cost, and care. Here is how to narrow the search and what to confirm before you commit.

sections.h2: How do I find a CCRC near me?
sections.body: Start by listing your must-haves: a location close to family, an entrance-fee budget, the contract type you want, and the care levels offered on campus. Search by metro area, then tour two or three communities in person. Ask each for its latest financial disclosure statement, confirm it is licensed in your state, and check whether it holds CARF accreditation — a respected quality mark held by fewer than 15% of CCRCs. Our advisors can build a shortlist near you and explain each community's contract. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What should I ask when touring a nearby CCRC?
sections.body: On a tour, ask about the entrance fee and whether it is refundable, the monthly fee and its history of increases, what triggers a move to a higher care level, and how that move affects your costs. Request the most recent financial disclosure and occupancy rate, since a CCRC's financial health protects your future care. Ask about staffing ratios in assisted living and skilled nursing. A licensed senior advisor can give you a question checklist — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How do I find a continuing care retirement community near me?
faqs.a: Decide your budget, location, and needed care levels, then search by metro area and tour two or three campuses. Review each community's financial disclosure, confirm state licensing, and check for CARF accreditation. For a free shortlist near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How many CCRCs are near me?
faqs.a: Availability varies widely by region. The U.S. has roughly 1,900 CCRCs total (LeadingAge), concentrated in states like Pennsylvania, California, Florida, and Ohio. Some metro areas have a dozen or more, while rural areas may have few. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to find communities in your area.

faqs.q: What does a CCRC near me cost?
faqs.a: Entrance fees commonly range from about $40,000 to over $2 million, with many between $300,000 and $600,000, plus monthly fees of roughly $2,000 to $6,000. Local real estate values strongly affect pricing. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for area-specific guidance.

faqs.q: Should I tour a CCRC before deciding?
faqs.a: Yes. Tour at least two or three communities in person. Compare residences, dining, activities, and care areas, and ask staff and current residents about life there. Always review the financial disclosure statement and residency agreement before signing. An advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you prepare.

faqs.q: Can you help me compare CCRCs near me for free?
faqs.a: Yes. 1-800-MEDIGAP offers free, no-pressure guidance for seniors and families. We help you compare nearby communities by entrance fee, contract type, and care levels, and review how Medicare and Medigap fit in. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to get started.

page.metaDescription: What is a CCRC? A continuing care retirement community offers independent living through skilled nursing on one campus. Learn how they work — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: A CCRC (continuing care retirement community), also called a Life Plan Community, is a senior housing campus combining independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing in one place, so residents age in place as needs change. There are about 1,900 CCRCs in the U.S. (LeadingAge). Learn more by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.heroSubhead: Wondering what a CCRC is? A continuing care retirement community lets seniors live independently now and access more care later — all on one campus. Get free, plain-English answers by calling 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: A CCRC is one of the most comprehensive senior living options. Here is what it is, how it works, and who it suits best.

sections.h2: What does CCRC stand for?
sections.body: CCRC stands for continuing care retirement community. The name describes its core promise: continuing care across a continuum of levels on one campus. A resident may move in to independent living while fully active, then later use assisted living, memory care, or skilled nursing as health needs evolve — without leaving the community. Many providers now call these Life Plan Communities, a term LeadingAge introduced in 2015. CCRCs typically charge a one-time entrance fee plus monthly fees that cover housing, services, and a share of future care. To learn how one works, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: Who is a CCRC right for?
sections.body: A CCRC suits seniors who want to settle in one place and avoid the stress of moving again as their health changes. It is especially valuable for couples with different care needs, since both can stay on the same campus. CCRCs work best for those who can afford the entrance fee and monthly costs and who value predictability and on-site care. They may be less ideal for seniors needing immediate intensive care or wanting a short-term arrangement. To see if a CCRC fits your situation, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What does CCRC stand for?
faqs.a: CCRC stands for continuing care retirement community. It is a senior housing campus that combines independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing in one location, allowing residents to age in place. Many are now called Life Plan Communities.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a CCRC and a nursing home?
faqs.a: A nursing home provides only skilled nursing care. A CCRC includes skilled nursing plus independent living, assisted living, and memory care on one campus. Residents typically move in while independent and use higher care levels only if and when they need them.

faqs.q: How does a CCRC work?
faqs.a: You pay a one-time entrance fee and ongoing monthly fees. You start in independent living and transition to assisted living, memory care, or skilled nursing on the same campus as your needs change. Your contract type determines how much higher-level care costs later.

faqs.q: Is a CCRC the same as assisted living?
faqs.a: No. Assisted living is a single level of care. A CCRC offers assisted living as one of several levels — alongside independent living, memory care, and skilled nursing — so residents can move between levels without changing communities.

faqs.q: Does Medicare cover a CCRC?
faqs.a: Medicare does not pay CCRC entrance or monthly fees. It does cover medically necessary care delivered within a CCRC, such as hospital stays and short-term skilled nursing rehab. A Medicare Supplement plan can cover the gaps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to review your coverage.

page.intro: Life Plan Community is the updated term for a CCRC. Here is what the name means and how to find one near you.

sections.h2: What is a Life Plan Community?
sections.body: A Life Plan Community is a continuing care retirement community (CCRC). LeadingAge introduced the term in 2015 to better convey the model's purpose: planning for your whole future in one place. The community combines independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing on a single campus, so residents transition between care levels without relocating. The terms are interchangeable — if you see "Life Plan Community" or "CCRC," they describe the same housing-and-care model. To explore Life Plan Communities near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: How do I find Life Plan Communities near me?
sections.body: Search by your metro area, then compare communities on entrance fee, monthly cost, contract type, and the care levels available on campus. Tour two or three in person, request each community's latest financial disclosure statement, and confirm it is licensed in your state. CARF accreditation is a strong quality indicator. Our senior advisors can build a shortlist of Life Plan Communities near you and explain the contracts in plain English. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free help.

faqs.q: Are Life Plan Communities the same as CCRCs?
faqs.a: Yes. "Life Plan Community" is the modern term for a continuing care retirement community (CCRC), adopted by LeadingAge in 2015. Both describe a campus offering independent living, assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing so residents can age in place.

faqs.q: How do I find Life Plan Communities near me?
faqs.a: Search by metro area, then compare communities on entrance fee, monthly cost, contract type, and care levels. Tour two or three, review financial disclosures, and confirm state licensing. For a free shortlist near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How much do Life Plan Communities cost?
faqs.a: Entrance fees commonly range from about $40,000 to over $2 million, with many between $300,000 and $600,000, plus monthly fees of roughly $2,000 to $6,000. Costs vary by location, residence, and contract type. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for guidance.

faqs.q: Why did CCRCs change their name to Life Plan Communities?
faqs.a: LeadingAge introduced the term "Life Plan Community" in 2015 after research found the older name confusing. The new name emphasizes proactive planning for one's future rather than a reaction to declining health. The underlying model is unchanged.

faqs.q: Does Medicare pay for a Life Plan Community?
faqs.a: Medicare does not cover entrance or monthly fees, which are housing costs. It does cover medically necessary care within the community, like hospital stays and short-term rehab. A Medicare Supplement plan can cover the gaps. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.

page.intro: CCRC pricing can look complicated. Here is a plain-English breakdown of entrance fees, monthly fees, and what drives the total.

sections.h2: How much does a CCRC cost?
sections.body: A CCRC has two main costs. The entrance fee is a one-time payment commonly ranging from about $40,000 to over $2 million, with many communities between $300,000 and $600,000. The monthly fee, typically $2,000 to $6,000, covers housing, maintenance, dining, amenities, and a portion of future care. Costs depend on location, residence size, whether the entrance fee is refundable, and your contract type. Type A (Life Care) contracts cost the most upfront but cap care-related increases later. To get a cost estimate for communities near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What do CCRC monthly fees include?
sections.body: Monthly fees generally cover your residence, property maintenance, some or all meals, housekeeping, utilities, transportation, wellness programs, and access to on-campus amenities. Depending on your contract type, the monthly fee may also include or discount future assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing. Fees usually rise modestly each year with inflation and operating costs, so ask each community for its fee-increase history. Understanding exactly what is bundled helps you compare communities accurately. A licensed advisor can help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: How much does a CCRC cost?
faqs.a: A CCRC charges a one-time entrance fee — commonly $40,000 to over $2 million, with many between $300,000 and $600,000 — plus monthly fees of roughly $2,000 to $6,000. Contract type, location, and residence size drive the total. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for a personalized estimate.

faqs.q: What is included in CCRC monthly fees?
faqs.a: Monthly fees typically cover your residence, maintenance, dining, housekeeping, utilities, transportation, and amenities. Depending on contract type, they may also include or discount future assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing. Fees usually rise modestly each year.

faqs.q: Why do CCRC costs vary so much?
faqs.a: Costs vary by location, residence size, amenities, whether the entrance fee is refundable, and contract type. Type A (Life Care) contracts prepay future care and cost more upfront, while Type C contracts charge less initially but full price for care later. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

faqs.q: Do CCRC monthly fees increase over time?
faqs.a: Yes. Monthly fees typically rise each year with inflation and operating costs, often a few percent annually. Ask each community for its fee-increase history before committing, since predictable increases signal sound financial management. An advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help you review.

faqs.q: Does insurance help with CCRC costs?
faqs.a: Medicare and Medigap do not pay entrance or monthly fees, but they cover medically necessary care within the community. Long-term care insurance may offset some assisted living or nursing costs. To review how your coverage applies, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.quickAnswer: A CCRC entrance fee is a one-time payment, commonly $300,000 to $600,000 (and ranging from about $40,000 to over $2 million), that secures your residence and helps prepay future care. It may be refundable or non-refundable depending on your contract. To understand entrance-fee options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.heroSubhead: The CCRC entrance fee is often the biggest decision in choosing a community. We explain what it buys, refund options, and how it affects monthly costs — free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: The entrance fee is central to how CCRCs work. Here is what it covers, why it varies, and how refund options change the math.

sections.h2: What is a CCRC entrance fee?
sections.body: A CCRC entrance fee is a one-time, upfront payment you make when moving in. It secures your residence and, depending on the contract, prepays a portion of your future assisted living, memory care, or skilled nursing. Entrance fees commonly fall between $300,000 and $600,000 but range from about $40,000 to over $2 million based on location, residence size, and refund terms. A higher entrance fee often means lower monthly fees and more prepaid future care. Many seniors fund it by selling a home. To understand entrance fees near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

sections.h2: What does the CCRC entrance fee pay for?
sections.body: The entrance fee does more than reserve your residence. In Type A and Type B contracts, part of it prepays future higher-level care, which is why those contracts cost more upfront but limit later care increases. The fee may also be partially refundable — many communities offer plans returning 50%, 75%, or 90% to you or your estate. Non-refundable (declining-balance) plans cost less initially but return nothing over time. Reading the residency agreement closely is essential. A licensed advisor can explain the trade-offs — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What is a CCRC entrance fee?
faqs.a: A CCRC entrance fee is a one-time upfront payment that secures your residence and, depending on the contract, prepays a portion of future care. It commonly ranges from $300,000 to $600,000 and may be refundable or non-refundable. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to learn more.

faqs.q: What does a CCRC entrance fee cover?
faqs.a: It reserves your residence and, in Type A and Type B contracts, prepays part of your future assisted living, memory care, or skilled nursing. A higher entrance fee usually means lower monthly fees and more prepaid care. Refund terms vary by contract.

faqs.q: How much is a typical CCRC entrance fee?
faqs.a: Entrance fees commonly range from $300,000 to $600,000, though they span roughly $40,000 to over $2 million depending on location, residence size, and refund options. Refundable plans cost more upfront. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for area-specific figures.

faqs.q: Can I get my CCRC entrance fee back?
faqs.a: Sometimes. Many CCRCs offer refundable contracts returning 50%, 75%, or 90% of the fee to you or your estate, usually after your unit is re-occupied. Declining-balance plans cost less upfront but refund nothing over time. Always read the residency agreement.

faqs.q: How do people pay the CCRC entrance fee?
faqs.a: Most seniors fund the entrance fee by selling their home, since the amount is similar to home equity for many. Others use savings, investments, or proceeds from downsizing. A financial advisor can help you plan. For guidance on the bigger picture, call 1-800-MEDIGAP.

page.metaDescription: What's the average CCRC entrance fee? Industry data points to roughly $300K-$400K, with a wide range. See what affects it — free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: The average CCRC entrance fee is estimated around $300,000 to $400,000, though individual fees range from about $40,000 to over $2 million depending on location, residence size, and refund terms (AARP and industry data). Refundable contracts push the average higher. For real figures near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.heroSubhead: Curious about the average CCRC entrance fee? Averages help set expectations, but your real cost depends on location and contract. We explain it free — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Averages are a useful starting point but hide a wide range. Here is what typical CCRC entrance fees look like and what moves the number.

sections.h2: What is the average CCRC entrance fee?
sections.body: Industry and AARP figures put the average CCRC entrance fee in the range of roughly $300,000 to $400,000, but this masks enormous variation. Actual fees run from about $40,000 at modest communities to well over $2 million at luxury campuses in high-cost areas. The biggest drivers are local real estate values, residence size, the level of prepaid future care in the contract, and whether the fee is refundable. Because averages vary so much, comparing real quotes near you matters more than any single number. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for area-specific figures.

sections.h2: What affects whether you pay above or below average?
sections.body: You will likely pay above the average entrance fee if you choose a refundable contract (returning 50% to 90% later), a larger residence, a Type A Life Care contract that prepays care, or a community in a high-cost metro. You will pay below average with a smaller unit, a non-refundable declining-balance plan, a Type C fee-for-service contract, or a community in a lower-cost region. Knowing which levers you control helps you budget realistically. A licensed advisor can model the options — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: What is the average CCRC entrance fee?
faqs.a: Industry and AARP data put the average CCRC entrance fee around $300,000 to $400,000, though individual fees range from about $40,000 to over $2 million. Location, residence size, contract type, and refund terms cause the wide spread. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP for local figures.

faqs.q: Why is there such a wide range in entrance fees?
faqs.a: Fees vary with local real estate values, residence size, the amount of prepaid future care, and whether the fee is refundable. A luxury refundable unit in a high-cost city can exceed $2 million, while a modest non-refundable unit may be under $100,000.

faqs.q: Do refundable contracts have higher average fees?
faqs.a: Yes. Refundable contracts, which return 50% to 90% of the entrance fee to you or your estate, carry noticeably higher upfront fees than declining-balance plans. You pay more now in exchange for getting much of it back later. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare.

faqs.q: Is the average entrance fee rising?
faqs.a: Entrance fees generally trend upward with real estate values and construction costs, similar to home prices. Because averages vary widely by region and contract, the most reliable approach is to gather current quotes from communities near you. An advisor at 1-800-MEDIGAP can help.

faqs.q: How does the average entrance fee compare to monthly fees?
faqs.a: The entrance fee is a large one-time payment averaging $300,000 to $400,000, while monthly fees of roughly $2,000 to $6,000 are ongoing. Generally, a higher entrance fee buys lower monthly fees and more prepaid care. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh the trade-off.

children.keyword: ccrc type a vs type b contract

page.metaTitle: CCRC Type A vs Type B Contract | 1-800-MEDIGAP
page.metaDescription: CCRC Type A vs Type B contract: Life Care vs Modified. Compare upfront cost, future care pricing & risk. Free senior guidance — call 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.h1: CCRC Type A vs Type B Contract
page.quickAnswer: A CCRC Type A (Life Care) contract charges higher upfront and monthly fees but locks in little or no cost increase when you need more care — essentially prepaid care. A Type B (Modified) contract costs less initially and includes a set amount of higher-level care, then charges discounted rates. To choose between them, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.heroSubhead: Comparing CCRC Type A vs Type B contracts? The choice shapes both your cost and your financial risk as care needs grow. We explain it in plain English — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Type A and Type B contracts handle future care costs very differently. Here is how they compare and which seniors each suits.

sections.h2: What is the difference between Type A and Type B CCRC contracts?
sections.body: A Type A (Life Care) contract charges the highest upfront and monthly fees but guarantees that assisted living, memory care, and skilled nursing cost little or nothing extra later — you effectively prepay future care and transfer the risk to the community. A Type B (Modified) contract costs less initially and includes a defined amount of higher-level care (for example, a set number of days), after which you pay discounted rates. Type B balances moderate upfront cost with some future protection. The right choice depends on your health outlook and finances. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to compare.

sections.h2: Which contract type is right for me?
sections.body: Type A suits seniors who want maximum predictability and protection against high future care costs, and who can afford larger upfront and monthly fees — it works like prepaid long-term care. Type B fits those who want lower initial costs and are comfortable sharing some future care expense, often a middle-ground choice. Your decision should weigh family health history, life expectancy, and how much financial risk you want to retain. A licensed advisor can model both scenarios against your budget. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) for free, no-pressure guidance.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a Type A and Type B CCRC contract?
faqs.a: A Type A (Life Care) contract charges higher upfront and monthly fees but caps future care costs — essentially prepaid care. A Type B (Modified) contract costs less initially, includes a set amount of higher-level care, then charges discounted rates. Type A shifts more risk to the community.

faqs.q: Is a Type A CCRC contract worth the higher cost?
faqs.a: It can be for seniors who value predictability and want protection against high future care costs, since Type A works like prepaid long-term care. Those in good health with strong longevity may benefit most. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to weigh the trade-off for your situation.

faqs.q: What does a Type B CCRC contract include?
faqs.a: A Type B (Modified) contract includes a defined amount of higher-level care — for example, a set number of assisted living or nursing days — at little or no extra cost, after which you pay discounted rates. It costs less upfront than Type A.

faqs.q: Which CCRC contract type has the lowest upfront cost?
faqs.a: Type C (Fee-for-Service) has the lowest entrance fee, but you pay full market rate for care as you use it. Between Type A and Type B, Type B has lower upfront costs than Type A. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare all three.

faqs.q: How do I decide between Type A and Type B?
faqs.a: Weigh your health outlook, family longevity, and how much financial risk you want to keep. Type A maximizes predictability at higher cost; Type B balances cost and protection. A licensed advisor can model both against your budget — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

page.metaDescription: Are CCRC entrance fees refundable? Often yes — plans return 50%, 75%, or 90%. Learn how refunds work before you sign. Free help at 1-800-MEDIGAP.
page.quickAnswer: It depends on the contract. Many CCRCs offer refundable entrance-fee plans returning 50%, 75%, or 90% to you or your estate, usually after your unit is re-occupied. Declining-balance (non-refundable) plans cost less upfront but refund nothing over time. To compare refund options near you, call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.heroSubhead: Wondering if CCRC entrance fees are refundable? Many are — but the terms vary widely and affect your upfront cost. We explain refund options for free. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
page.intro: Refundability is one of the most important details in a CCRC contract. Here is how refund plans work and what to watch for.

sections.h2: Are CCRC entrance fees refundable?
sections.body: Many CCRC entrance fees are partially or substantially refundable, but it depends entirely on the contract you choose. Refundable plans commonly guarantee 50%, 75%, or 90% of the fee back to you or your estate, typically paid after your residence is re-sold or re-occupied. In exchange, these plans carry higher upfront fees. Declining-balance (non-refundable) plans cost less initially but the refundable portion shrinks over time — often to zero after a few years. Because terms and payout timing vary, reading the residency agreement closely is essential. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427) to understand your options.

sections.h2: How do CCRC entrance-fee refunds work?
sections.body: With a refundable contract, the community returns the guaranteed percentage — 50%, 75%, or 90% — when you move out, pass away, or your unit is re-occupied, depending on the agreement. Two key details matter: the refund percentage and the trigger for payment. Some contracts pay the refund only after a new resident occupies your unit, which can mean a delay. Others pay on a fixed timeline. Ask each community to spell out the refund amount, timing, and conditions in writing. A licensed advisor can help you compare the fine print — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).

faqs.q: Are CCRC entrance fees refundable?
faqs.a: Often, yes — but it depends on the contract. Many CCRCs offer refundable plans returning 50%, 75%, or 90% of the entrance fee to you or your estate, usually after the unit is re-occupied. Non-refundable declining-balance plans cost less upfront but refund nothing over time.

faqs.q: How much of a CCRC entrance fee is refundable?
faqs.a: Refundable contracts commonly return 50%, 75%, or 90% of the entrance fee. Higher refund percentages carry higher upfront fees. Declining-balance plans refund a shrinking amount that often reaches zero after a few years. Call 1-800-MEDIGAP to compare options.

faqs.q: When is a CCRC entrance fee refund paid?
faqs.a: It varies by contract. Many communities pay the refund only after your unit is re-sold or re-occupied, which can cause a delay. Others pay on a fixed timeline. Always confirm the refund trigger and timing in writing before signing the residency agreement.

faqs.q: Does my estate get the CCRC refund if I pass away?
faqs.a: With a refundable contract, yes — the guaranteed percentage is typically paid to your estate after your residence is re-occupied. This is a key reason some seniors choose refundable plans despite higher upfront fees. Confirm the exact terms in your residency agreement.

faqs.q: Is a refundable or non-refundable CCRC plan better?
faqs.a: Refundable plans preserve more wealth for heirs but cost more upfront; non-refundable plans free up cash now but return nothing later. The right choice depends on your finances and estate goals. A licensed advisor can help — call 1-800-MEDIGAP (1-800-633-4427).
